coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) incorporated dual-layer hollow fibers for pervaporative dehydration of ethanol

coredata.dc:description: Pervaporative ethanol dehydration is a promising approach to concentrate ethanol from biofuel stocks. In this study, we have fabricated high performance dual-layer cellulose triacetate (CTA)/Ultem hollow fiber membranes incorporated with a small amount of superhydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via the immiscibility induced phase separation (I2PS) method. The flow rates of the inner- and outer-layer dopes were firstly optimized. Subsequently, various small amounts of CQDs were added into the inner- and outer-layer dope solutions to explore their impacts on membrane structure and separation performance. It was found that the incorporation of a reasonably small amount of CQDs, whether to the inner or outer layer of I2PS membranes, could significantly enhance the mechanical properties and pervaporation performance by altering their structures. The most effective membrane containing 0.2 wt% CQD in the outer layer (0.2CQD_OL) demonstrated exceptional pervaporation performance. Using an 85 wt% ethanol feed solution, this membrane achieved a total flux of 4306 ± 130 g m−2h−1 (equivalent to a water permeance of 33.28 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1) and a water content of approximately 95 wt% (equivalent to a mole-based selectivity of 153) in the permeate over a 172 hr long-term test. A comparison with literature underscores the superior performance of this newly developed I2PS membrane, particularly in terms of water permeance. In summary, the CQD-embedded CTA/Ultem HF membranes demonstrate substantial potential for industrial applications in the pervaporative ethanol dehydration.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pervaporative ethanol dehydration is a promising approach to concentrate ethanol from biofuel stocks. In this study, we have fabricated high performance dual-layer cellulose triacetate (CTA)/Ultem hollow fiber membranes incorporated with a small amount of superhydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via the immiscibility induced phase separation (I2PS) method. The flow rates of the inner- and outer-layer dopes were firstly optimized. Subsequently, various small amounts of CQDs were added into the inner- and outer-layer dope solutions to explore their impacts on membrane structure and separation performance. It was found that the incorporation of a reasonably small amount of CQDs, whether to the inner or outer layer of I2PS membranes, could significantly enhance the mechanical properties and pervaporation performance by altering their structures. The most effective membrane containing 0.2 wt% CQD in the outer layer (0.2CQD_OL) demonstrated exceptional pervaporation performance. Using an 85 wt% ethanol feed solution, this membrane achieved a total flux of 4306 ± 130 g m−2h−1 (equivalent to a water permeance of 33.28 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1) and a water content of approximately 95 wt% (equivalent to a mole-based selectivity of 153) in the permeate over a 172 hr long-term test. A comparison with literature underscores the superior performance of this newly developed I2PS membrane, particularly in terms of water permeance. In summary, the CQD-embedded CTA/Ultem HF membranes demonstrate substantial potential for industrial applications in the pervaporative ethanol dehydration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the Energy Information Administration (EIA), the annual consumption of biofuels increased almost tenfold from 2000 to 2021 [1]. Among biofuels, ethanol is the most popular one because of its diverse applications. It not only can replace gasoline but also has a relatively lower carbon dioxide emission than burning traditional gasoline. In 1975, the National Alcohol Program initiated by Brazil, subsidized investments in ethanol production for sugarcane producers and lowered ethanol prices at gas stations to develop flexible fuel vehicles capable of running on any proportion of gasoline and ethanol. By 1985, 96 % of new cars sold in Brazil had full ethanol engines. Moreover, between 2006 and 2017, the prevalence of flexible fuel vehicles increased significantly, rising from 10.8 % in 2006 to 62.7 % in 2017 [2]. In addition, Volkswagen in 2003 introduced “flex-fuel” cars capable of running on any blend of gasoline and ethanol. Afterwards, other manufacturers followed it. This solidified the ethanol's position as the primary energy source in Brazil. Globally, ethanol holds promise as a renewable energy source for the future [3,4].

Among the numerous methods to concentrate ethanol from biofuel stocks, such as pressure swing absorption, liquid–liquid extraction, adsorption, and membrane pervaporation (PV), PV maintains an unshakable position due to its advantages as follows: (1) it can directly break the azeotropic mixture; (2) it incurs relatively fewer equipment costs because it can be easily installed at the downstream of a distillation column; (3) it is energy efficient; and (4) it has a small footprint. Therefore, many researchers have fabricated pervaporation membranes using various membrane materials and configurations [5–11], such as flat sheet membranes, hollow fiber membranes, and tubular membranes to separate alcohol solutions and produce high purity ethanol.

Hydrophilic polymers, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [10], chitosan (CS) [12], sodium alginate (SA) [13], and polyamide (PA) [13], have been widely studied for pervaporation because of their capability to improve the permselectivity of water. However, membranes fabricated with hydrophilicity polymers lack mechanical strength and stability in aqueous solutions because of swelling. These hydrophilic membranes thus need to be cross-linked, which inevitably affected the permeance. Additionally, the extra cross-linking step increases the production costs.

Flat sheet membranes are commonly used in the research stage because they can be easily fabricated [14–16], chemically modified [10,13,17,18], and quickly tested. However, hollow fiber membranes offer some merits [6,19] because (1) they have a large surface area per unit membrane mass; (2) they are less susceptible to contamination and fouling; (3) they have a self-support structure capable of providing internal vacuum channels for the PV system. All these factors may result in PV hollow fiber modules with a higher packing density and a longer lifetime. Modifications of asymmetric single-layer hollow fiber membranes such as dip-coating [20], cross-linking [8,21,22], plasma treatment [23], interfacial polymerization [11,21,22], and annealing [24,25] are often needed to enhance the separation perforrmance. However, these kinds of modifications make the production process complicated for scaling up. Therefore, the dual-layer hollow fiber spinning technology was developed in the late 1970 s because of the following advantages [25,26]: (1) It keep all the merits of single-layer hollow fiber membranes; (2) materials that have excellent performance but poor mechanical properties can be spun with the aid of the supporting layer in dual-layer spinning; (3) the dosage of expensive functional materials can be signicantly reduced because of the dual-layer configuration; (4) An ultra-thin dense selectivity layer (i.e., 10 μm or less) can be formed during the co-extusion. These facts not only allow to substantially reduce material costs but also enable to fabricate multi-layer hollow fiber membranes with a more straightforward technique.

Since dual-layer hollow fiber spinning is more complex, one must understand the spinning variables to prevent the delamination phenomenon in dual-layer hollow fiber membranes. The delamination between the inner and outer layers not only weakens the overall mechanical properties, but also lower the separation performance and shortens the membrane lifetime. Li et al. [27] was one of the pioneers to study the effects of spinning parameters such as inner- and outer-layer dope flow rates, bore flow rate, air–gap distance, take-up speed, external coagulant, and coagulant temperature on dual-layer morphology and delamination phenomenon [28]. They identified which spinning parameters could overcome the delamination phenomenon. For example, varying spinneret temperature from 25 to 60 °C could not effectively minimize it, while increasing air–gap distance and take-up speed might minimize it. In addition, excessive tightening of the outer layer on the inner layer during the phase inversion could deform the inner layer, while increasing the inner-layer dope flow rate might improve the dual-layer morphology. The bore fluid chemistry also played an important role to eliminate the delamination because a strong internal coagulant could faciltate the precipitation of the inner skin and minimize the delamination

To lower the material cost of dual-layer hollow fiber membranes, most researchers opt to employ an expensive functional material as the outer layer to achieve a desired selectivity, while choosing a cheap material with good mechanical properties as the inner supporting layer [25,29]. Because the outer selective layer of hollow fibers in this design would directly contact with the hot feed with a high alcohol concentration, a swelling phenomenon may occur in the outer selective layer that leads to deteriorate the separation performance. In addition, the outer-layer thickness plays an important role in determining the overall permeance. One must reduce its thickness while maintaining the separation performance. However, the thinner the outer-layer thickness results in the higher the solvent-induced swelling phenomenon.

To overcome both issues, Ong et al. [30–32] invented a method called immiscible induced phase separation (I2PS) to fabricate high performance dual-layer hollow fiber membranes for pervaporation. In their studies, different inner and outer layer materials have been investigated in the I2PS process, and the best pervaporation performance can be achieved by using the pair of cellulose triacetate (CTA) and Ultem as the outer and inner layers, respectively. Ultem with a backbone of polyetherimide has been studied for pervaporative dehydration since 1990 s because of their good thermal, chemical, mechanical stabilities, as well as good permselective properties [33–36]. To produce effective pervaporation membranes using Ultem, dope concentrations must be at or above the critical concentration [37]. However, these membranes face a trade-off relationship between selectivity and permeability when increasing the dope concentration. Additionally, direct contact between the dense selective layer and the feed alcohol causes swelling in the selective layer, thereby reducing the selectivity. Cellulose acetate possesses excellent physicochemical properties, including high mechanical strength and exceptional hydrolytic stability [38]. Chao et al have developed composite membrane based on a CA subtract via interfacial polymerization and used it to dehydrate ethanol feed solution. They took advantage of the hydrophilic group on CA to promote the adsorption of the aqueous amine solution on the membranes [39]. During the I2PS process, a thin dense-selective layer would be formed on the outer surface of the inner Ultem layer, while the outer CTA layer of the I2PS membranes took the responsibility to protect the thin dense-selective layer from the hot biofuel stock.

Porous nanomaterials such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs) and Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks(ZIFs) may have potential to improve the pervaporation performance because of their high porosity [40–44], Xu et al. attempted to incorporate MOFs (UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles) into I2PS membranes and found effectiveness to enhance the separation performance because of the lower transport resistance and the higher affinity among nanoparticles, water and membranes. Their hollow fiber comprising 0.2 wt% UiO-66-NH2 in the outer layer possessed an impressive flux of 2667 g m−2h−1 and its permeate had 95.00 wt% water, (i.e., a mole-based selectivity of 152 at 50 °C using a feed consisting of 85 wt% water and 15 wt% ethanol.

In addition to using porous materials to reduce mass transfer resistance, the other way to enhance permeability is by manipulating membrane wettability, which is a crucial parameter to determine the pervaporative dehydration performance [7]. Most of the pervaporative membranes follow the solution-diffusion model [45,46], which divide the separation process into dissolution, diffusion, and desorption. The strong wettability of the membranes can improve the sorption selectivity in the dissolution step, thereby enhancing the pervaporative performance. In our previous study, we have successfully synthesized superhydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a simple pyrolysis process using citric acid as the precursor [47]. We aim to explore in this work if CQDs can be incorporated into I2PS membranes to change the wettability of the membranes and enhance the pervaporative dehydration performance. The rationale to choose CQDs is because of their abundant surface hydrophilicity functional groups, and others unique and favorable properties, such as easy synthesis from inexpensive raw materials, zero dimensionality (i.e., high surface area per volume), and ease of conjugating chemistry. In this work, the optimization of dope flow rates in both the inner and outer layers of I2PS membranes were firstly examined, followed by the studies of CQDs loading in either or both inner and outer layers on membrane morphology and separation performance. Finally, a long-term test was performed to ensure the stability of the newly fabricated CQDs embedded CTA/Ultem dual-layer membranes.

Cellulose triacetate (CTA, CA-436-80S) was kindly provided by Eastman. Ultem® 1010 polyetherimide (PEI) was purchased from SABIC Innovative Plastics. CTA and Ultem were vacuum dried at 90 °C overnight before usage. Citric acid anhydrous (CA, >99.5 %) was purchased from SHOWA chemical Industry Co., Ltd., and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ACS reagent) pellets were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Moreover, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5 %, Echo Chemical Co., LTD), methanol (99.0 %, Echo Chemical Co., LTD), n-hexane (≥95.0 %, Echo Chemical Co., LTD) and ethanol (95.0 %, Echo Chemical Co., LTD) were solvents used in this study. The N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99 %, Macron) were used for dope solution preparations. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses and characterizations of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A bottom-up method was utilized to synthesize CQDs via direct pyrolysis of citric acid following the literature [48,49]. In brief, 5 g of citric acid was loaded into a Teflon lined autoclave reactor and heated at 210 °C for 3 hr. The solid citric acid powders were transformed into a brown liquid with a paste-like texture. Subsequently, the cooled brown solution was neutralized to pH 7 using 5 M NaOH and then diluted with deionized water to achieve a final volume of 100 mL. Afterwards, the solution was filtered using dialysis tubing (MWCO = 2 kDa, Spectrum LifeSciences, LLC) for two days, followed by filtration with a 0.22 μm PVDF (KWO-YI CO., LTD) microfilter to remove any incompletely reacted large precursors and impurities. The filtered solution was further concentrated by a rotary evaporator (RE202, Yamato) to reduce the total volume and subsequently freeze-dried (Alpha 2–4 LSCplus, Martin Christ).

The synthesized CQDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (FETEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). The TEM images were further analyzed by ImageJ software to obtain the particle size distribution. In addition, the quantum confinement effect of the CQDs was characterized via photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL-02, UniPLUX) with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm [50,51].

The compositions of all polymer dopes were tabulated in Table 1 . Following our previous work [30,31], the I2PS membranes fabricated in this study consisted of a 26 wt% Ultem and a 10 wt% CTA as the inner and outer layer materials, respectively. The selective layer was formed at the interface between Ultem and CTA layers due to their immiscibility [30]. Various amounts of CQDs were loaded to the inner or the outer layer separately to study their effects. In brief, a pre-measured amount of CQDs was dispersed in NMP using a homogenizer (T25, IKA) at 8000 rpm for 2 h. Thereafter, Ultem or CTA was gradually added into the CQDs/NMP mixture and vigorously stirred overnight. Subsequently, the dope solution was loaded into the syringe pump (500D, TELEDYNE ISCO) and degassed overnight before spinning.

The dual-layer hollow fiber (HF) membranes were spun via the dry-jet wet-spinning method [52]. The dual-layer spinneret was purchased from KASEN NOZZLE INC., and its specifications were shown in Figure S1. All spinning parameters are illustrated in Table 2 . As shown in Figure S2, the outer-layer, inner-layer polymer dopes as well as the bore fluid were co-extruded through a triple orifice spinneret. The co-extruded HFs first passed through an air gap distance of 1.5 cm and subsequently precipitated in an external coagulant bath. The nascent free-fall HFs were collected by a take-up roller without applying any additional force to stretch the fibers (i.e., a free-fall condition). The HFs were immersed in a tap water bath for one day to remove the residual NMP solvent. Subsequently, the nascent fibers underwent solvent exchange with methanol three times, followed by n-hexane one time. Each solvent exchange step lasted for 30 min. Finally, the HF membranes were air-dried at room temperature and preserved in a desiccator before uses.

To conduct the pervaporation experiments, a HF membrane was sealed in a handmade module consisting of two Swagelok stainless steel three-way connectors and a 1/4″ polytetrafluoroethylene (PFA) tube (Figure S3(a)). Each module comprised a single HF with an effective length of 14 cm.

Pervaporation performance of HFs was evaluated using a lab-scale pervaporation apparatus reported previously [30,31]. As depicted in Figure S3(b), a 2 L stainless steel tank was used to store the feed solution, where an 85 wt% ethanol aqueous solution was delivered by a recirculation pump to circulate from the shell side of the HF at a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min. The downstream pressure of the lumen side was kept lower than 1 mbar by a vacuum pump. The system was conditioned for 1 h to ensure it reached the steady state ahead of sampling the permeate. Afterward, the permeate was collected using a liquid nitrogen cold trap. The flux was determined by weighing the condensed permeate in a specific time interval, while the permeate composition was analyzed by gas chromatography (SHIMADZU, Nexis GC-2030) equipped with a SH-I-624Sil MS capillary column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).

The morphologies of HF membranes were observed by a field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6500F, JEOL). The samples were prepared by fracturing the CTA/Ultem dual-layer membranes in liquid nitrogen and coated with platinum using a sputtering device (JFC-1200, JEOL).

ATR-FTIR analyses were conducted to study the interactions between CQDs and polymers (i.e., CTA or Ultem). Flat sheet membranes embedded with CQDs, using both CTA and Ultem, were prepared through the conventional nonsolvent induced phase separation method. First, CQDs were dispersed in 10 wt% CTA or 26 wt% Ultem dope solutions at the concentrations specified in Table 1, reflecting the loading amounts in the I2PS membranes. Subsequently, these dope solutions were cast on a glass plate using a casting knife with a gap height of 200 um, followed by the same phase inversion and solvent exchange protocols detailed in the Section 2.3.

In addition to the CTA and Ultem flat sheet membranes with low CQD concentrations (0 to 0.3 wt%), membranes with a higher CQD concentration of 1 wt% were also prepared. This was adopted to meticulously examine whether any new characteristic peaks emerged due to the synergistic interaction between CQDs and the CTA and Ultem polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The flat sheet membranes of CTA and Ultem with a CQD concentration of 1 wt% were further used for TGA and DSC tests. The thermal stability of membranes was assessed using TGA (TGA 550, TA Instruments) over a temperature range of 40–700 °C at a heating rate 10 °C/min under inert N2 atmosphere [53,54]. The glass transition and enthalpy changed behaviors were measured by DSC (Discovery DSC 25P, TA Instruments) with a heating rate of 10 °C/min from room temperature to 250 °C. Membranes were cut into small pieces and weighted (3–5 mg) prior to placing them in DCS pans.

The wettability of I2PS membranes was assessed by measuring the DCAs of water using a tensiometer (DCAT 25, DataPhysics Instruments) through a force tensiometry method at 25 °C [55,56]. In brief, a HF was affixed to a fiber holder that connecting to the force sensor of the instrument. Subsequently, the fiber was automatically immersed into a DI water and then withdrawn from it. The advancing water contact angle and the receding water contact angle were automatically calculated by the software. The advancing contact angles provide the repel ability between membrane surface and water at the initial state, which represent the most truthy wettability behavior of membranes. Since immersed membranes absorbed water inside their pores, the corresponding receding contact angles were reduced [57]. The advancing contact angles were used to discuss and compared the surface hydrophilicity [55,56].

The mechanical properties of HF membranes were assessed using an Instron universal testing system (Instron). The outer diameter of each HF specimen was measured by SEM images with the aid of ImageJ software. At least 5 specimens were tested to obtain the averaged Young’s modulus (MPa), maximum tensile stress (MPa), and elongation at break (%) [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of dope flow rates of inner and outer layers for I2PS membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The cross-section morphologies of I2PS membranes with various inner-layer dope flow rates ranging from 1.4 to 1.8 mL/min are displayed in Fig. 1 . The cross-section images show that the I2PS membranes spun at various inner-layer dope flow rates are all uniform and have a hollow concentric structure (Fig. 1 (a1), (b1), and (c1)). With an increase in inner-layer dope flow rate, the wall of I2PS membranes becomes thicker (Fig. 1 (a2), (b2), and (c2)) [59]. A clear boundary between Ultem and CTA can be observed, indicating the formation of a dense selective layer due to the I2PS mechanism between the two incompatible polymers (Fig. 1 (a3), (b3), and (c3)). In addition, the thickness of the outer layer (i.e., CTA layer) becomes thinner with an increase in inner-layer dope flow rate. This may arise from a bigger inner-layer die swelling that results in a larger inner-layer diameter at a higher inner-layer dope flow rate. The die swelling phenomenon is due to the relaxation of random-coil polymeric chains after passing through an annular spinneret [60]. Because the inner-layer diameter becomes larger with an increase in inner-layer dope flow rate, the thickness of the CTA outer layer becomes thinner.

On the other hand, varying the outer-layer dope flow rate from 0.1 to 0.3 mL min−1 result in a similar hollow concentric structure (Fig. 2 (a1), (b1), and (c1)). Because these hollow fibers were spun from the same inner-layer dope flow rate of 1.8 mL/min, and the outer-layer dope flow rates are much smaller compared to the inner-layer dope flow rate (i.e., 0.1 to 0.3 vs. 1.8 mL min−1), there are apparently no significant changes in the overall wall thickness of these hollow fiber membranes (Fig. 2 (a2), (b2), and (c2)). However, the outer-layer thickness increased significantly from 0.32 μm to 3.72 μm when the outer-layer dope flow rate increased from 0.1 to 0.3 mL min−1, which may impose a higher mass transfer resistance during pervaporation tests (Fig. 2 (a3), (b3) and (c3)).

The optimal inner-layer and outer-layer dope flow rates of I2PS membranes were determined by their pervaporation performance for ethanol dehydration. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the total flux slightly increases 11 % from 2768 g m−2h−1 to 3079 g m−2h−1 when the inner-layer dope flow rate rises from 1.4 mL min−1 to 1.8 mL min−1. This may result from the thinning of the outer layer thickness at a higher inner-layer dope flow rate that leads to reduce the mass transfer resistance for pervaporation. Interestingly, the water concentrations in the permeate of all membranes are not compromised by the reduced outer layer thickness, they all remain at approximately 95 wt%. This is mainly ascribed to the unique characteristics of I2PS membranes where the selectivity layer is formed at the outer surface of the inner Ultem layer (i.e., at the interface between Ultem and CTA layers). Because the dual-layer hollow fiber spun at the inner-layer dope flow rate of 1.8 mL min−1 has the highest flux and rejection, it is considered to be the optimal and used for the following experiments.

The effects of outer-layer dope flow rate on pervaporation performance is depicted in Fig. 3b. The total flux is the highest at 6330 g m−2h−1 when the outer-layer dope flow rate is 0.1 mL min−1. However, its water content in the permeate is only 54 wt%, significantly lower than other membranes because of an incomplete coverage of the outer protective layer (Fig. 2 (a3)), as observed in the previous study [31]. When the outer-layer dope flow rate slightly increases to 0.2 mL min−1, the total flux drops to 3079 g m−2h−1 but the water content in the permeate jumps to 94.3 wt%. This indicates that a continuous CTA layer is formed on top of the Ultem layer at this outer-layer dope flow rate. Because a continuous outer CTA layer plays an indispensable role in the I2PS method, it serves as dual purposes. Not only does it induce the formation of a selective Ultem layer at the interface but also act as a protective layer to prevent the Ultem selective layer from direct contact with the feed solution (i.e., minimizing its membrane swelling) [30]. A further increase in outer-layer dope flow rate to 0.3 mL min−1 leads to a thicker outer layer with a higher mass transfer resistance, thereby reducing the total flux. Therefore, the optimal outer-layer dope flow rate is determined to be 0.2 mL min−1 after considering its satisfactory separation performance and the total flux.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the I2PS membranes with various CQD concentrations in the inner and outer layers
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of adding various CQD concentrations into the inner and outer layers were evaluated and discussed in this section. The synthesized CQDs have an average size of 1.81 nm and exhibit strong emission at a wavelength of 466 nm, attributed to the quantum confinement effect [61] (Figure S4 (a)-(c)). In addition, the CQDs are extremely hydrophilic due to the presence of abundant carboxylic acid groups, as evidenced by the OH (3425 cm−1), C = O (1573 cm−1), and C–O (1397 cm−1) functionalities in the FTIR spectrum (Figure S4 (d)) [47,48].

To understand the interactions among CQDs, Ultem and CTA and their effects on membrane formation, both flat-sheet Ultem and CTA membranes containing different CQD concentrations were also cast for comparison. ATR-FTIR spectra were utilized to investigate their chemical interactions. Although low CQD concentrations (0.05 ∼ 0.3 wt%) were used in dual-layer hollow fiber spinning, slightly high CQD concentrations up to 1 wt% were employed to cast the flat sheet membranes in order to observe the interactions more clearly. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the phthalimide carbonyl stretching, centered at 1722 cm−1 in pure Ultem membranes, slightly shifts to 1721 cm−1 at low CQD concentrations of 0.05–0.2 wt%. This peak further shifts to 1715 cm−1 and becomes broader at a higher CQD concentration of 1 wt%. The shift to lower frequencies and the asymmetric broadening of the carbonyl peak indicate the formation of hydrogen bonds, altering the carbonyl stretching modes [62]. A similar shift and broadening of carbonyl characteristic peak can be observed in CTA membranes when elevating the CQD concentration (Fig. 4b).

In terms of dope viscosity, Table 1 shows that the addition of CQDs increases the viscosity of CTA and Ultem spinning dopes. This increase in viscosity can be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CQDs and the polymers. Additionally, the superhydrophilic nature of the CQDs may also absorb water from the surroundings, potentially elevating the dope viscosity. However, the viscosity increments are not significant due to the low CQD concentrations (i.e., 0.05 ∼ 0.3 wt%) and the very small size of CQDs (i.e., the average particle size is 1.81 ± 0.35 nm as shown in Figure S4).

The formation of hydrogen bonds when CQDs are added to CTA and Ultem is illustrated in Figures S5(a) and S5(b). The abundant carboxylic acid functional groups on the CQDs act as crosslinkers, forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds that reinforce the membrane. To further elucidate the interactions between CQDs and the polymers, TGA and DSC analyses were conducted. The TGA results show that the onset temperature (Tonset) of both Ultem and CTA shifts to higher values in the presence of CQDs (Figures S5(c) and S5(d)). Additionally, the DSC curves of the CQD-containing membranes exhibit a stronger endothermic heat flow (Figures S5(e) and S5(f)). Both analyses further confirm that additional bonds are formed between CQDs and the polymer chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different CQD concentrations on hollow fiber morphology
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 demonstrates the cross-sectional morphology of the I2PS membranes as a function of CQD concentration in the inner-layer Ultem dope. In general, adding CQDs to the inner layer does not alter the membrane morphology (Fig. 5 (a1)-(d1)). As discussed previously, the addition of CQDs only slightly increases the dope viscosity. However, the change in viscosity is still far below the viscosity at the critical polymer concentration (Figure S6). Therefore, the macrostructure of the inner layer is not affected by the incorporation of CQDs, where the large finger-like macrovoids persist in all membranes (Fig. 5 (a2)-(d2)) due to the non-solvent intrusion [30]. Despite showing no impact on membrane macrostructure, delamination between the inner and outer layers occurs when the CQDs concentration reaches 0.2 wt% (Fig. 5 (d2)).

In the pristine I2PS membrane without CQDs, numerous small and teardrop-like microvoids exist in the Ultem layer, pointing outwards, as indicated by the arrow in Fig. 5 (a3). These small microvoids are gradually eliminated as the CQD concentration in the Ultem layer increases (Fig. 5 (b3)-(d3)). As discussed previously, CQDs can form various bonding with both CTA and Ultem. It is speculated that the CQDs in the Ultem layer could mediate the immiscibility between Ultem and CTA, thereby reducing the formation of small microvoids near the interface between CTA and Ultem layers. On the other hand, the formation of the large finger-like macrovoids in the inner Ultem layer is mainly caused by the nonsolvent intrusion from the external coagulant [30]. The presence of very low CQD concentrations (up to 0.2 wt%) in the inner layer is not enough to affect the overall phase inversion mechanism, thus the large finger-like macrovoids persist in all membranes.

On the other hand, it is intriguing to observe that the addition of CQDs to the outer-layer CTA dope somehow results in a different microstructure of the inner Ultem layer. As displayed in Fig. 6 (a1)-(d1) and (a2)-(d2), adding 0.1 to 0.3 wt% CQDs to the CTA layer has no direct impact on the macrostructure of the I2PS membranes, all membranes have similar finger-like macrovoids in the inner Ultem layer. However, as demonstrated in Fig. 6 (a3)-(d3) when being observed at a high magnification of x5,000, the inner Ultem layer apparently becomes more porous as the CQD concentration in the outer layer increases. Because water must transport across the CTA layer before reaching the nascent inner Ultem layer. The presence of CQDs in the outer CTA layer may attract water [48] as well as retard the water transport rate, thus slow down the nonsolvent intrusion into the Ultem layer. Consequently, the delayed demixing results in a more porous inner Ultem layer.

In summary, the incorporation of CQDs into the inner Ultem layer may potentially moderate the immiscibility between Ultem and CTA layers and affect the interface dynamics during the I2PS process. This leads to a reduction of small microvoids at the interface. On the other hand, the addition of CQDs to the outer CTA layer promotes water absorption and retention, which in turn, delays phase inversion within the inner Ultem layer. This delayed phase inversion results in a more porous Ultem microstructure, demonstrating the significant role of CQDs on membrane morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQD incorporation on mechanical properties of I2PS membranes
ce:section.ce:para: During the pervaporation, the hollow fiber membranes must have reasonably good mechanical properties to withstand the stresses under vacuum from the lumen side. As shown in Table 3 , the addition of CQDs significantly enhances mechanical properties of I2PS membranes such as Young’s modulus and maximum tensile stress, regardless of whether they are added to the inner or outer layer. For example, the pristine membrane without CQDs has a Young's modulus of 253.95 MPa and a maximum tensile stress of 7.48 MPa. With the addition of 0.2 wt% CQDs to the inner layer, these values increase to 447.29 MPa and 10.89 MPa, respectively. Similarly, the addition of 0.2 wt% CQDs to the outer layer raises the Young's modulus to 417.27 MPa and the maximum tensile stress to 12.93 MPa.

The mechanical properties of I2PS membranes are primarily determined by the structure of the inner Ultem layer, where macrovoids are the major weak points affecting the overall mechanical properties. Although finger-like macrovoids are present in all membranes, SEM images show that their structure are slightly different. In the pristine membrane, the macrovoids are numerous, randomly distributed, and vary in size, as depicted in Fig. 5 (a2). When being subjected to an external stress, these nonuniform macrovoids fail to evenly distribute the load, leading to lower mechanical properties. In contrast, with the addition of CQDs to either the Ultem or CTA layer, the finger-like macrovoids become more uniform and their number decreases (e.g., in Fig. 5 (d2) and Fig. 6 (d2)). The fewer and evenly distributed macrovoids in the Ultem layer lead to significantly improve Young’s modulus and tensile strength.

Besides structural differences, adding CQDs to the inner Ultem layer could potentially reinforce the membrane through the formation of strong hydrogen bonds, as evidenced by the increasing Young’s modulus with higher CQD concentrations. However, these strong interactions between CQDs and Ultem also restrict the mobility of polymer chains, leading to a significant reduction in elongation at break from 43.6 % to 19.3 %. In contrast, when CQDs are added to the outer CTA layer, the elongation at break stays almost similar to that of the pristine membrane because the polymer chains of the inner Ultem layer remain flexible and bear most of the stresses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQD loading on pervaporative dehydration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a shows the ethanol dehydration performance of the I2PS membranes with various CQD loadings in the inner Ultem layer. The pristine membrane has a total flux of 3079 g m−2h−1 and a water content of approximately 94 % in the permeate. The total flux consistently increases with an increase in CQD loading in the inner layer, reaching 4516 g m−2h−1 and a water content of ∼ 95 % at a CQD loading of 0.1 wt%. However, a further increase in CQD loading to 0.2 wt% results in a higher total flux to 5147 g m−2h−1 but a much lower water content of 41 %, possibly due to the delamination phenomenon shown in Fig. 5 (d2). Moreover, the reduced immiscibility between the Ultem and CTA layers due to a high CQD loading may lead to a less compact selective layer, thus impairing the separation performance.

In contrast, the separation performance varies less significantly at higher CQD loadings in the outer CTA layer. As displayed in Fig. 7b, the water content in the permeate remains between 93 to 95 wt% when the CQD loading in the CTA layer ranges from 0.1 to 0.3 wt%. The total flux initially increases to 4305 g m−2h−1 at a CQD loading of 0.2 wt%, then decreases to 3197 g m−2h−1 possibly due to a higher mass transfer resistance at a higher CQD loading of 0.3 wt%. These results indicate that incorporation of a small amount of CQDs into the I2PS membranes, whether in the inner Ultem or outer CTA layer, effectively increases the total flux without compromising separation performance.

For curiosity, I2PS membranes with CQDs in both layers simultaneously were also fabricated to check if the pervaporation performance could be further improved. As summarized in Table S1, simultaneous addition of CQDs to both layers led to severe delamination or a low water content of less than 90 wt% in the permeate. Consequently, it can be concluded that adding CQDs to a single layer, whether CTA or Ultem layer, yields better results. As shown in Fig. 7, two I2PS membranes with (1) 0.1 wt% CQD in the inner Ultem layer (referred to as 0.1CQD_IL) and (2) 0.2 wt% CQD in the outer CTA layer (referred to as 0.2CQD_OL) have superior pervaporation performance in their individual groups. Because the inner dope flow rate is much larger than the outer dope flow rate (Table 2), the latter would consume a much less amount of CQDs than the former when scaling up the production of I2PS membranes. Thus, the I2PS membrane of 0.2CQD_OL is selected for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of feed temperature on pervaporative dehydration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 a plots the total flux as a function of feed temperature from 30 °C to 70 °C. The ethanol/water separation performance remains stable when the feed temperature is below 60 °C because the water content in the permeate maintains at 95 wt%. However, the water content in the permeate drops slightly when the feed temperature reaches 70 °C possibly due to higher chain movement and membrane swelling at elevated temperatures. Therefore, the water and ethanol fluxes at feed temperatures of 30–60 °C were further used to calculate the activity coefficients of water and ethanol, respectively (Fig. 8b and 8c). By employing the Arrhenius equation, the activation energy for water transport is 25.14 kJ/mol, while that for ethanol is 37.83 kJ/mol. The positive activation energy values indicate that increasing the feed temperature can promote the permeation flux [30–32,63]. In addition, because water transport has a lower activation energy (i.e., a smaller energy barrier) than ethanol, the former has a lower transport resistance than the latter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ethanol concentration on pervaporation performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 illustrates the ethanol dehydration performance as a function of feed ethanol concentration from 75 wt% to 95 wt%. As the feed ethanol concentration increases, the water fugacity at the feed side decreases, resulting in a decline in the total flux [11,44,64]. When the feed ethanol concentration is raised to 95 wt%, the water concentration in the permeate drops to 86.21 wt%. This may result from a lower water fugacity and the membrane swelling in a solution containing a high ethanol concentration [44,64].

Membranes with long-term stability are crucial for their practical applications. Therefore, the ethanol dehydration performance of the 0.2CQD_OL membrane was monitored for 172 h at 50 °C, using an 85 wt% ethanol aqueous solution as the feed. The ethanol concentration in the feed was measured and adjusted periodically to ensure a consistent feed concentration over the entire period. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the membrane demonstrates a very stable total flux of 4300 g m−2h−1, with the water content in the permeate consistently maintains at 95 wt%.

Table 4 benchmarks the PV performance of various membranes reported in literature. The flux and water content were standardized into permeance and mole-based selectivity to minimize the influence of operating conditions. Comparing to polymer membranes or MMMs, the dual-layer CTA/Ultem hollow fiber membrane prepared by the I2PS method already has a higher water permeance with a reasonable selectivity [30]. For instance, the MOF-801/CS membrane [65] had a water permeance of 7.62 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1, and the PA/MZIF-8 membrane [42] showed a water permeance of 12.03 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1. By incorporating CQDs into the outer CTA layer, the water permeance was further increased from 14.18 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1 to 33.28 mol m−2h−1 kPa−1. It is worth highlighting that the production of CQDs is simple and cost-effective compared to other materials used in similar studies, such as graphene, ZIFs, or MOFs [16,32,42,65–67]. Additionally, the membrane's durability is confirmed by the long-term test. In conclusion, the CQD-embedded CTA/Ultem membranes outperform traditional PV membranes with their superior water permeance and reasonable selectivity. Combined with their cost-effectiveness and ease of fabrication, these attributes make them a promising candidate for large-scale ethanol dehydration applications.

In this study, we have developed high-performance CTA/Ultem dual-layer I2PS membranes incorporated with superhydrophilic CQDs for pervaporative ethanol dehydration. The following conclusions can be drawn:
(1)
The mechanical properties of I2PS membranes are significantly reinforced by the incorporation of CQDs, whether embedding them in the inner or the outer layer.
(2)
The ethanol dehydration performance of I2PS membranes significantly benefits from targeted CQD incorporation into a single layer. However, a high CQD loading in the inner Ultem layer can increase flux but compromise water content due to delamination. In contrast, variations of CQD loadings in the outer CTA layer maintain stable water content, suggesting a less severe impact on performance.
(3)
The best pervaporative dehydration results are achieved by adding CQDs to either the inner or outer layer individually. The I2PS membrane with 0.2 wt% CQD in the outer CTA layer has been identified as the most promising due to its balanced performance and material efficiency.
(4)
The CQD-embedded CTA/Ultem I2PS membrane outperforms other membranes in literature for ethanol dehydration, with a superior water permeance and reasonable selectivity.
(5)
The long-term stability of the 0.2CQD_OL membrane exhibits a stable flux of 4306 ± 130 g m−2h−1, with a water content of 94.97 wt% in the permeate over 172hr. These results strongly indicate that 0.2CQD_OL is an excellent candidate for use in the pervaporation process.
